channel.title: Seamus Logan
channel.description: MP for Aberdeenshire North and Moray East

item.title: UK Government has failed to meet WASPI Campaigners Despite Pre-Election Pledges
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: The UK Government has failed to meet with the WASPI campaigners in the last two years since they were elected and since the Parliamentary Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO) published its report. The PHSO findings entitled ‘Women’s State Pension Age: Our findings on injustice and associated issues’ recommended compensation due to DWP maladministration while the Conservatives […]
item.content:encoded:
The UK Government has failed to meet with the WASPI campaigners in the last two years since they were elected and since the Parliamentary Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO) published its report.

The PHSO findings entitled ‘Women’s State Pension Age: Our findings on injustice and associated issues’ recommended compensation due to DWP maladministration while the Conservatives were in power at Westminster in March 2024.

Pre the 2024 General Election, many Labour MPs and party candidates pledged their support for the WASPI women’s plight, but on taking office as the next UK Government, failed to accept the findings of the PHSO and refused compensation. Subsequently, there is no record of any meeting with WASPI women in an official capacity at Westminster since September 2024.

I believe there is a real disconnect between the Government’s warm words for the WASPI campaign pre-election and its lack of action post-election on implementing the recommendations of the PHSO. As has been noted on numerous occasions, it is unprecedented for a UK Government to refuse to accept a Parliamentary Ombudsman’s findings; to add insult to injury, it is disingenuous to infer support for a campaign before voters take to the polls to then reverse your position once in power.

Perhaps this is why Ministers at the DWP are so reluctant to meet these campaigners in person. I’m sure it would be very uncomfortable for them to explain face to face to women so badly failed by their government why they should not receive compensation and yet victims of other scandals should. Because Westminster has been plagued by injustices in how members of the public have been mistreated by public bodies in the last few years. Given the Government is finally in the process of acting to compensate those affected by the Infected Blood scandal, the Post Office scandal and now, hopefully the nuclear test veterans as well, it is well beyond time that the Labour government recognise that WASPI women’s need compensation needs addressed too.

In Parliament this week in reply to my suggestion for Labour to U-turn on another of their policies turned bad, the Pensions’ Minister highlighted how compensating WASPI women was never an actual Labour manifesto pledge. I fear this makes the UK Government look even more hypocritical in their numerous photo op poses with WASPI campaigners only to back pedal once they could make a real difference in government to the injustice suffered by these 1950s born women.

The UK Government need to relocate their backbone, meet with WASPI women, listen to their concerns and suggestions for ways to implement a full and comprehensive compensation scheme as recommended by the PHSO, and make good on the pledges they made so readily before they came to power.

item.title: Scottish Hauliers Hit Breaking Point due to Westminster ‘Fuel Tax Hike’
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: The Westminster government’s shambolic handling of fuel prices is pushing haulage firms in North-east Scotland to the brink by volatile costs and a lack of support. The Westminster government is preparing to reintroduce fuel duty in stages from September, which I believe will act like a stealth tax on the backbone of the Scottish economy. […]
item.content:encoded:
The Westminster government’s shambolic handling of fuel prices is pushing haulage firms in North-east Scotland to the brink by volatile costs and a lack of support.

The Westminster government is preparing to reintroduce fuel duty in stages from September, which I believe will act like a stealth tax on the backbone of the Scottish economy.

In my opinion, it’s is a total farce that Scotland – the nation that fills Westminster’s coffers with oil revenues – is being forced to pay some of the highest pump prices in Europe. 

Local hauliers are telling me they simply cannot plan for next month, let alone next year, because of volatile price fluctuations.

The SNP has been clear: We need a Fuel Duty Regulator now. We need the VAT on duty scrapped. 

Instead, we have a Westminster government that is happy to find money for steelworks in the south of England while standing idly by as the Grangemouth refinery closes its doors. 

By allowing our refining infrastructure to vanish while hiking taxes on fuel, the Westminster government is effectively sabotaging Scottish industry. I have written to the Minister today to demand they stop the planned duty increases and finally give our hauliers the stability they deserve.

item.title: Building Workforce Opportunities and Skills in CCUS
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: I’ve given my backing to the Carbon Capture and Storage Association’s skills initiative launched at a recent parliamentary event marking the publication of a paper focused on new opportunities for careers in the Carbon Capture and Storage industry. This paper entitled ‘Delivering CCUS: Building the Workforce & Skills for the UK’s Industrial Transition’, features case studies […]
item.content:encoded:
I’ve given my backing to the Carbon Capture and Storage Association’s skills initiative launched at a recent parliamentary event marking the publication of a paper focused on new opportunities for careers in the Carbon Capture and Storage industry.

This paper entitled ‘Delivering CCUS: Building the Workforce & Skills for the UK’s Industrial Transition’, features case studies showcasing how investment in CCUS is creating opportunities for the next generation of energy and industry professionals. 

The event brought together cross-party parliamentarians with industry leaders delivering CCUS projects across the UK or keen to make the most of developing new opportunities alongside apprentices and graduates working across this sector.

The paper’s recommendations include unlocking greater policy certainty from Government to allow clear routes to market, ensuring investors, employers and training providers can be confident in backing a wider pipeline of projects and the workforce needed to deliver them. Getting this right means creating real career opportunities in industrial communities for young professionals with estimates of around 15,000 direct roles and a further 50,000 jobs if the CCUS sector is fully developed, not to mentions safeguarding existing industrial employment.

As MP for Aberdeenshire North and Moray East, investment in our young workforce by unlocking key opportunities in the CCUS sector is crucial to our growth. It matches the ambition and commitment I’ve encountered with local educational establishments such as the North-east Scotland College, employers like SSEN and ambitious businesses linked to this industry.

Since becoming an MP, I’ve been pushing for the UK Government to show support for the Acorn Carbon Capture and Storage Project at St Fergus near Peterhead in the same way that has been afforded to projects in Humberside and Teeside as discussed at this launch. The recurring theme of these discussions centres on creating a much more certain policy landscape to boost investor confidence, protect jobs, and build a workforce and skills base that is fit for the future. We live in challenging and uncertain economic times, making it all the more important to build a resilient workforce and a fully supported carbon capture, utilisation and storage sector as part of our decarbonisation drive.

It was inspirational to meet with the young apprentices, including some local to my constituency, to hear about their experiences working in this sector and their ambitions for the future. We must work together to do all we can to protect and deliver successful career pathways for these young professionals across engineering, manufacture, and construction as part of the wider Just Transition.

Photograph with Nic Braley, General Manager at Storegga at the Acorn Project, St Fergus

item.title: Logan Joins New Cross-Poverty Prevention Initiative as Founding Member
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: As one of 20 founding supporters of a new Parliamentary Poverty Prevention Caucus, I’ll be working together with cross party parliamentarians to prevent poverty and reduce inequality. In Aberdeenshire North and Moray East, it is estimated that 2264 households are living on below the average income, underlining the urgency of coordinated action at both a […]
item.content:encoded:
As one of 20 founding supporters of a new Parliamentary Poverty Prevention Caucus, I’ll be working together with cross party parliamentarians to prevent poverty and reduce inequality.

In Aberdeenshire North and Moray East, it is estimated that 2264 households are living on below the average income, underlining the urgency of coordinated action at both a local and national level.

The Caucus, supported by Taxpayers Against Poverty in partnership with Compassion in Politics, aims to bring together MPs from across political parties to promote practical, forward-looking solutions that reduce hardship, strengthen communities, and deliver long-term economic value.

As part of my commitment, I’ve signed a unified parliamentary pledge:

“I pledge to prioritise policies preventing poverty and inequality, acting with integrity, respect, and compassion in Parliament and my constituency.”

The Parliamentary Poverty Prevention Caucus will work alongside parliamentarians, experts and other groups, to advance policies designed to prevent the causes of poverty.

A launch event is planned in Westminster later this year, in collaboration with parliamentary groups focused on poverty and compassionate politics.

Alongside the parliamentary pledge, Taxpayers Against Poverty has also launched a public petition calling on the government to make tackling priority one of its key priorities.

More information here: https://www.taxpayersagainstpoverty.org.uk/stop-funding-failure

item.title: Challenging Assumptions on Multiple Sclerosis for MS Awareness Week 2026
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: I’m supporting the MS Society’s campaign to challenge assumptions on Multiple Sclerosis during MS Awareness Week 2026 (20th-26th April). The Charity are keen to highlight how MS affects everyone differently, with symptoms ranging from mild to severe, and often a huge variation in how these symptoms manifest themselves from day to day. Often the symptoms […]
item.content:encoded:
I’m supporting the MS Society’s campaign to challenge assumptions on Multiple Sclerosis during MS Awareness Week 2026 (20th-26th April).

The Charity are keen to highlight how MS affects everyone differently, with symptoms ranging from mild to severe, and often a huge variation in how these symptoms manifest themselves from day to day. Often the symptoms are invisible, affecting how people see, think, or feel; other times they can affect how people walk or move.

This neurological condition affects more than 150,000 in the UK, with 17,406 people in Scotland living with MS. Issues with accessing treatment, care, and financial support to manage the condition are reported by those living with MS as well as misconceptions in the public about the real-life impacts. Almost half of those surveyed by the MS Society report being questioned or challenged for using accessible facilities such as Blue Badge parking spaces, while 1 in 4 say that fear of judgement has prevented them from leaving the house or applying for a job.

In discussions with the MS Society, the theme that comes up constantly is the lack of understanding around this progressive condition, which, in many cases, eventually leads to severe disability and limited life. This is why I’m pleased to support the Charity’s work in raising awareness and challenging public misconceptions on MS where no two days can bring the same symptoms and challenges dealing with pain or exhaustion as well as the mental load can leave people feeling isolated and misunderstood.

It’s vital that the public and health care professionals are aware of the how MS manifests so differently in people. Nearly all the people surveyed by the MS Charity (95%) said that they experience at least one invisible symptom like fatigue, brain fog or pain and around half reporting that they were made to feel like their invisible symptoms weren’t real.

I want to ensure that any of my constituents in Aberdeenshire North and Moray East who are living with MS know that I stand with them and will continue to speak up about the challenges they face in Parliament and with my Scottish Government colleagues.

Photograph with Susannah Copson, Senior Public Affairs Officer at the MS Society UK.

item.title: Discussions with Falkland Islands Govt on Right to Self-determination and NE Supply Chain Opportunities
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: At a meeting with the Falkland Islands Government in the Houses of Parliament, I was pleased to pledge my support for Falkland Islanders’ right to self-determination. I met with representatives of the Falkland Islands Government last week to hear the latest news and updates from the Falklands and to learn more about Islanders’ right to […]
item.content:encoded:
At a meeting with the Falkland Islands Government in the Houses of Parliament, I was pleased to pledge my support for Falkland Islanders’ right to self-determination.

I met with representatives of the Falkland Islands Government last week to hear the latest news and updates from the Falklands and to learn more about Islanders’ right to self-determination, which is a fundamental right enshrined in article one, paragraph two of the United Nations Charter.

In 2013, the Falkland Islands held an internationally observed sovereignty referendum, in which 99.8 per cent of voters, on a turnout of 92 per cent, voted in favour of remaining an internally self-government Overseas Territory of the United Kingdom. 

The Falkland Islands are an internally self-governing and economically self-sufficient UK Overseas Territory located in the South Atlantic. Strategically placed at the gateway to Antarctica, the Islands are home to over 3,600 people.

I was very pleased to meet with representatives of the Falkland Islands Government and to hear more about the Falklands and Islanders’ important right to self-determination. I am proud to support the Falklands in their desire to remain part of the UK family, and I was interested to learn more about the Falklands’ thriving economy, beautiful environment and their strategic importance to the UK.

I’m currently working closely with the Falkland Islands Government on supply chain opportunities for the North-east following the announcement of a $2.1billion investment in development of the Islands’ offshore resources.

I was delighted to visit the Falkland Islands last year as a guest of the Falklands Islands Government, is keen that the expertise and supply chain available in the North-east is well-placed to take advantage of potential opportunities.

I am very keen that the existing well of expertise and personnel built up over many years of supplying the North Sea industry can take full advantage of what I expect will be a range of opportunities available as a result of this investment decision.

We are still in the early stages of the Just Transition and while work towards that continues to build, we still need oil and gas and the supply chain still needs to have meaningful opportunities to bid for contracts.

  • Photograph shows Seamus Logan MP with the Falkland Islands Government Representative to the UK & Europe, Richard Hyslop.

item.title: Local Charity Nets Over £250 from MP’s Charity Bet on Scottish Grand National
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: I’m pleased to have played my part in scoring a £250 win for local charity Men United last Saturday. I was offered a charity bet at Ladbrokes by the Betting and Gaming Council, the industry body which works with member bookmakers, large and small, to raise standards and create a culture of safer gambling. I […]
item.content:encoded:
I’m pleased to have played my part in scoring a £250 win for local charity Men United last Saturday.

I was offered a charity bet at Ladbrokes by the Betting and Gaming Council, the industry body which works with member bookmakers, large and small, to raise standards and create a culture of safer gambling.

I teamed-up with members of Peterhead-based Men United to choose some selections for the Scottish Grand National, which took place on Saturday.  Favourite Kim Roque was placed third meaning Men United gain from an each-way bet plus £250.00.

I’m grateful to Christina Bain and her team at Ladbrokes in Peterhead for their support on Saturday while myself and members of Men United studied the form.

The Betting and Gaming Council does important work in the promotion of safer gambling amongst other things and my thanks go to them and Ladbrokes for supporting the bet, which will see over £250.00 go to Men United to support them in their work.

Note: Photograph shows (l to r): Sandy Garvock of Men United; Christina Bain of Ladbrokes; Kelly Dobbie and James Stephen of Men United; and Seamus Logan MP.

item.title: Justice for WASPI Women
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: I’ve formally signed a cross-party Parliamentary Early Day Motion (EDM) demanding that the Westminster government immediately compensate 1950s-born women impacted by changes to the State Pension age. The move intensifies the pressure on Westminster to address the long-standing WASPI (Women Against State Pension Inequality) scandal, which has left thousands of Scottish women in financial hardship.  […]
item.content:encoded:
I’ve formally signed a cross-party Parliamentary Early Day Motion (EDM) demanding that the Westminster government immediately compensate 1950s-born women impacted by changes to the State Pension age. 

The move intensifies the pressure on Westminster to address the long-standing WASPI (Women Against State Pension Inequality) scandal, which has left thousands of Scottish women in financial hardship. 

Despite the Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman confirming that maladministration occurred, the Westminster government has repeatedly refused to implement a fair compensation scheme for those affected.

The WASPI campaign has highlighted how poorly communicated increases to the State Pension age forced women into poverty, with many receiving little to no notice of the changes. 

The EDM condemns the Westminster government’s decision to ignore the Ombudsman’s recommendations and calls for immediate mediation with WASPI representatives to deliver overdue financial redress.

https://edm.parliament.uk/early-day-motion/65517/changes-to-state-pension-age-affecting-1950sborn-women

The treatment of WASPI women by successive Westminster governments is nothing short of a national disgrace. These women worked hard, paid their dues, and had the rug pulled from under them with almost no warning. 

This WASPI injustice is yet another clear example of why Westminster isn’t working for Scotland. For years, we have watched as Westminster ministers ignore the Ombudsman, ignore the campaigners, and ignore the clear moral obligation to pay the compensation the WASPI women deserve. 

It is increasingly obvious that Scotland’s pensioners would be better served if we had full control over our own pension policy. Instead of being an afterthought in a broken Westminster system, we could build a social security net that treats our 1950s-born women with the dignity and respect they earned. 

The WASPI generation has waited long enough—it’s time for Westminster to pay up or get out of the way.

item.title: Westminster is “Strangling” Scottish Tourism – I’m backing “Hospitality Together” Campaign
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: I’ve pledged full support for the Hospitality Together campaign, calling for a cut in VAT to 10% to help the North-east’s struggling tourism and hospitality sector. The move comes as hospitality businesses across Scotland face a “triple whammy” of soaring energy costs, labour shortages caused by Brexit and Westminster’s restrictive immigration policies, and a VAT […]
item.content:encoded:
I’ve pledged full support for the Hospitality Together campaign, calling for a cut in VAT to 10% to help the North-east’s struggling tourism and hospitality sector.

The move comes as hospitality businesses across Scotland face a “triple whammy” of soaring energy costs, labour shortages caused by Brexit and Westminster’s restrictive immigration policies, and a VAT regime that is significantly higher than most European competitors.

The North-east of Scotland is particularly vulnerable to these reserved policies, as the region relies heavily on seasonal tourism and a mobile workforce. 

The SNP has long advocated for the devolution of VAT powers to Holyrood that would incentivise growth rather than punish it.

I’m now calling on the Westminster government to heed the concerns of the 300+ businesses represented by Hospitality Together and implement an immediate fiscal intervention before the upcoming budget.

Hospitality is the economic lifeblood of many North-east communities. From our coastal cafes to our historic rural inns, these businesses often define our communities. 

It is a travesty that while the SNP Scottish Government works to support the sector through business rates relief and infrastructure investment, the Westminster government is effectively strangling them with a 20% VAT rate and damaging economic policies.

The Chancellor has been warned repeatedly that without a VAT cut to 10%, we could see more cherished local venues close their doors. It is time for Westminster to stop treating hospitality as a cash cow and start treating it as the vital economic engine it is.

item.title: Watch Out for Surge in Pet-Related Scams
item.dc:creator: Alison Anderson
item.description: I’m keen to warn my constituents of a surge in pet-related scams according to Santander’s latest Scamtracker results. Their data shows a 400% increase in the amounts stolen by criminals posing as legitimate pet sellers, throwing an important spotlight on this growing financial crime as well as serious concern for animal welfare. The Santander Quarterly […]
item.content:encoded:
I’m keen to warn my constituents of a surge in pet-related scams according to Santander’s latest Scamtracker results.

Their data shows a 400% increase in the amounts stolen by criminals posing as legitimate pet sellers, throwing an important spotlight on this growing financial crime as well as serious concern for animal welfare.

The Santander Quarterly Scamtracker analyses trends in Authorised Push Payment (APP) scam types reported each quarter, highlights the customers most affected, and identifies emerging threats. These insights provide a robust evidence base to support more coordinated efforts to combat pet fraud.

The results for January to March 2026 show an alarming increase in the amounts stolen from customers who believed they were purchasing a pet. Nearly £160,000 was stolen from customers responding to listings on social media and online selling platforms offering pets for sale, compared to £29,735 reported in Q4 2025. When looking more closely at the pet scams recorded, the bank’s data shows that women, and customers aged 46+ were most likely to be targeted.  

Common tactics used by scammers included asking for money up front as a deposit, requesting funds to cover travel to deliver the pet, or money to cover vet bills and immunisations. Claims received by the bank concerned a range of animals, from desirable breeds of dogs and cats through to parrots, reptiles, fish and horses.  

In total, more than £3.4 million was stolen via purchase scams in Q1 2026.  

Santander’s Scamtracker results make for alarming reading, and I want to alert my constituents to the risks of purchasing a pet online given the threat to their financial situation and also these poor animals welfare.

The Bank has shared tips to help avoid being scammed such as being wary of adverts on social media which is the most commonly used place for criminals to advertise fake pets; to ensure you do adequate background checks on the seller and pet such as doing a reverse image search to see if the animal has been listed elsewhere; to ask to see the animal in person before making any commitments and to always be cautious of upfront payments, or being asked to pay large deposits or vets bills.

If you are planning on getting a pet you are better to go to a reputable breeder or to adopt one of the many animals desperate for a second chance in our cat and dog homes across the country. Of course, online platforms should also be stepping up to the challenge by prohibiting sellers using their sites to try and sell animals in the first place. In the meantime, following Santander’s practical advice is a goodstarting point to stop scammers taking advantage of people and animals.
